1:
"Then drew near unto him all the publicans and sinners for to hear him." The witnesses note the throng of the outcast drawn to Christ and the grace it displays. Ambrose sets the scene in the flow of the Gospel: having taught us "not to prefer transitory things to eternal," and knowing "the frailty of man cannot keep a firm step in so slippery a world, the good Physician has shown you a remedy even after falling; the merciful Judge has not denied the hope of pardon." Theophylact draws out the reason: "this was His wont, for the sake whereof He had taken upon Him the flesh, to receive sinners as the physician those that are sick." The Gloss identifies the publicans as "those who collect or farm the public taxes, and make a business of following after worldly gain" — the most despised class of Jewish society, reckoned little better than the heathen.

The witnesses note the striking draw: it was the publicans and sinners, not the religious, who "drew near" to hear Christ — the very persons the Pharisees shunned now pressing about the Saviour.

So the witnesses make the verse the setting of the three great parables of grace. The lesson the expositors draw is the magnetism of Christ for the lost and the purpose of his coming. The publicans and sinners, the witnesses note, were the refuse of society in the Pharisees' eyes — tax-gatherers who oppressed their own people for Roman gain, and open transgressors of the law; yet these "drew near unto him for to hear him," finding in Christ what they found nowhere else: a teacher who did not spurn them. The expositors press the reason Theophylact gives: this was the very end of the incarnation, that the Physician might come to the sick, the Saviour to the lost; Christ took flesh precisely to seek and save such as these. Ambrose's framing, the witnesses observe, sets the chapter as the remedy for the fallen: the good Physician shows a cure even after the fall, and the merciful Judge holds out pardon to the penitent. The verse thus opens the chapter not with the righteous but with the ruined, and prepares for the murmuring of the Pharisees and the threefold vindication of grace that follows — the lost sheep, the lost coin, and the lost son. It teaches, the expositors note, that the drawing near of sinners to Christ is no scandal but the very glory of the gospel: he came for them, and they, sensing it, came to him; and the whole chapter unfolds to justify and magnify that reception of the lost which the self-righteous despised.
2:
"And the Pharisees and scribes murmured, saying, This man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them." The witnesses note the murmuring of the self-righteous and the grace hidden in their complaint. The grammatical-historical Barnes exposes their reasoning: "they affected to suppose that if Jesus treated sinners kindly he must be fond of their society, and be a man of similar character; they considered it disgraceful to eat with them... They would not suppose that he admitted them for the purpose of doing them good, nor remember that the very object of his coming was to call the wicked from their ways." Adam Clarke turns the accusation into a gospel: "Receiveth sinners — he receives them cordially, affectionately, takes them to his bosom, for so the word implies. What mercy! Jesus receives sinners in the most loving, affectionate manner, and saves them unto eternal life."

The witnesses note the irony: the very charge the Pharisees meant as a reproach — "This man receiveth sinners" — is in truth the highest praise of the Saviour, the summary of the gospel itself.

So the witnesses make the verse the murmuring that occasioned the parables and a window into the gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between the self-righteous contempt of the Pharisees and the saving love of Christ. The scribes and Pharisees, the witnesses note, "murmured" — not openly accusing, but muttering their disdain — at seeing Christ welcome the publicans and eat with them, for to their minds such association proved him no holy man but a companion of the wicked. The expositors press what their murmur betrayed: a heart that despised sinners and could not conceive that Christ received them to heal, not to share their sin; they judged the Physician by the company of his patients, blind to his purpose. Yet, the witnesses observe with Clarke, their very words of reproach are the gospel in miniature: "This man receiveth sinners" — yes, he does, and that is the wonder and glory of his grace; he receives them not coldly nor reluctantly, but cordially, taking them to his bosom, and saving them to eternal life. The expositors note that the Pharisees, in seeking to condemn Christ, unwittingly proclaimed the very truth on which every sinner's hope hangs. The verse thus sets the stage for the three parables, in which Christ will vindicate his reception of sinners and expose the loveless murmuring of those who, accounting themselves righteous, grudged the lost their Saviour — answering the charge "he receiveth sinners" not by denial but by three stories that make it the joy of heaven itself.
3:
"And he spake this parable unto them, saying..." The witnesses note that Christ answers the murmuring with parables, and the unity of the three. The Reformed Gill explains the threefold purpose: the parable was spoken "to the Pharisees, for their conviction and confutation; and to the publicans and sinners for their encouragement; and in vindication of himself" — and not this parable only, "but the other two, concerning the lost piece of money, and the prodigal son, which were said at this time, and on the same occasion." Wesley notes "three parables of the same import: for the sheep, the piece of silver, and the lost son, all declare, in direct contrariety to the Pharisees and scribes, in what manner God receiveth sinners." Robertson calls it "Christ's way of answering the cavilling of these chronic complainers," and cites Ragg's beautiful analysis: "The three Episodes form a climax: The Pasture — the House — the Home; the Herdsman — the Housewife — the Father; the Sheep — the Treasure — the Beloved Son."

The witnesses note that Christ does not rebuke the murmurers with argument but with stories — three parables that together vindicate his grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the introduction to the great trilogy of the lost. The lesson the expositors draw is the method and unity of Christ's answer. To the Pharisees' murmuring, the witnesses note, Christ replies not with a bare defence but with parables — winning images that disarm prejudice and reveal the heart of God; he "champions the lost and accepts the challenge and justifies his conduct by these superb stories" (Robertson). The three parables, the expositors press, are one in purpose, declaring "in what manner God receiveth sinners" (Wesley): all show something lost, sought, found, and rejoiced over, and so vindicate Christ's reception of the publicans and sinners as the very pattern of God's own joy over the recovered. Yet, the witnesses observe with Ragg, the three rise to a climax — from the sheep strayed in the open pasture, to the coin lost in the house, to the son alienated from the home; from the shepherd's search, to the housewife's diligence, to the father's embrace; each parable deepening the picture of grace until it reaches its height in the prodigal's welcome. The expositors note the threefold aim Gill names: to convict the Pharisees, to encourage the sinners, and to vindicate Christ himself. The verse thus opens the answer Christ gives to all who would grudge the lost their Saviour — an answer not of rebuke but of revelation, unfolding in three rising stories the joy of God over one sinner that repents.
4:
"What man of you, having an hundred sheep, if he lose one of them, doth not leave the ninety and nine in the wilderness, and go after that which is lost, until he find it?" The witnesses expound the shepherd's search. Adam Clarke draws out the allegory: "the whole flock of mankind, both Jews and Gentiles, belongs unto this Divine Shepherd; and it is but reasonable to expect that the gracious proprietor will look after those who have gone astray, and bring them back to the flock. The lost sheep is an emblem of a heedless, thoughtless sinner... No creature strays more easily than a sheep; none is more heedless." Lightfoot notes the "ninety-and-nine" as "a very familiar way of numbering and dividing amongst the Jews." The witnesses note the shepherd leaves the ninety and nine to seek the one — not despising the many, but bent on recovering the lost.

The witnesses note the appeal to common feeling: "what man of you" would not do this? — Christ grounds his pursuit of sinners in the instinct every shepherd owns.

So the witnesses make the verse the heart of the first parable: the shepherd's pursuit of the lost. The lesson the expositors draw is the seeking love of Christ for the straying sinner. The sheep, the witnesses note, is the apt emblem of the sinner: heedless, thoughtless, prone to wander, unable to find its own way back — none strays more easily, none is more helpless once lost. And the shepherd, the expositors press, is Christ, the Divine owner of the whole flock of mankind, who, when one sheep is lost, does not wait for it to return (it cannot) but "goes after that which is lost, until he find it" — the active, persevering pursuit of grace that seeks the sinner who could never seek himself. The witnesses observe the force of "until he find it": the shepherd does not search half-heartedly nor give up, but presses on until the lost is recovered — a picture of the unwearied, effectual love of Christ. The leaving of the ninety and nine, the expositors note, marks not contempt for the many but the special concern of love for the one in danger; and the appeal "what man of you" grounds the whole in the universal instinct of a shepherd's heart, that the Pharisees themselves must own. The verse thus answers their murmuring at the root: if any shepherd seeks his one lost sheep, how should not the Shepherd of souls seek his lost — receiving sinners not as a fault but as the very work of his shepherd-love, who came to seek and to save that which was lost.
5:
"And when he hath found it, he layeth it on his shoulders, rejoicing." The witnesses dwell on the tender recovery of the sheep. The Reformed Gill expounds the finding: "Christ finds his sheep in a most desolate condition, in the mire and clay of nature, in the paw of the roaring lion Satan, helpless, hopeless, wretched, and naked... dead in trespasses and sins"; and finding them, "he speaks life into them, calls them by name, takes them out of the pit of nature, rescues them from Satan, washes them, heals them, covers them with his robe of righteousness, and brings them home." Ellicott notes the vivid image of the shepherd laying the sheep "on his shoulders" — bearing it home himself, the helpless creature carried by the one who sought it.

The witnesses note the shepherd does not drive the found sheep before him, but lays it on his own shoulders and carries it, rejoicing — the picture of grace bearing the helpless sinner home.

So the witnesses make the verse the tender close of the recovery. The lesson the expositors draw is the gentleness and joy of Christ in saving the lost. The found sheep, the witnesses note, is weary, weak, unable to walk the long way home; and the shepherd, far from chiding or driving it, "layeth it on his shoulders" — taking the whole weight of the helpless creature upon himself and bearing it home. This, the expositors press, is the image of grace: Christ does not merely find the sinner and leave him to make his own way back, but carries him — bears him on his own strength, his own merit, his own shoulders — so that the salvation is wholly the Shepherd's work, the sheep contributing nothing but its lostness and its need. Gill's rich unfolding, the witnesses observe, names all that Christ does for the found: he gives life to the dead, rescues from Satan, cleanses, heals, clothes with his righteousness, and bears home — a whole salvation accomplished by the seeking, finding, carrying Shepherd. And the shepherd does it all "rejoicing": the recovery of the lost is his joy, not his burden, the very gladness the parable will press as the joy of heaven over one sinner that repents. The verse thus crowns the search with the tenderest stroke of grace — the lost sheep, found and helpless, borne home on the rejoicing Shepherd's shoulders — answering the Pharisees' cold contempt with the warm love of Christ, who counts it joy to carry the sinner home.
6:
"And when he cometh home, he calleth together his friends and neighbours, saying unto them, Rejoice with me; for I have found my sheep which was lost." The witnesses note the shepherd's home-coming and shared joy. The Reformed Gill expounds the "home": it is "either the church of God, which is Christ's house and home... or else heaven, which is Christ's Father's house and his own home, whither he brings his people; for they cannot go there alone and of themselves, but are brought by the power of divine grace." Ellicott notes the recurrence of "friends and neighbours" so soon after Luke 14:12 as "suggestive" — the shepherd calls others to share his gladness. Barnes cross-references the psalmist's cry, "I have gone astray like a lost sheep; seek thy servant" (Psalm 119:176).

The witnesses note the shepherd cannot keep his joy to himself, but gathers friends and neighbours to rejoice with him over the one sheep recovered.

So the witnesses make the verse the sharing of the shepherd's joy. The lesson the expositors draw is the communal, overflowing gladness that the recovery of one lost soul produces. The shepherd, the witnesses note, having borne the sheep home, does not rejoice in private but "calleth together his friends and neighbours, saying, Rejoice with me" — his joy is too great to contain, and he would have others share it. This, the expositors press, is the very heart of the parable's answer to the Pharisees: the finding of the lost is an occasion not of grudging but of festal joy, in which all who love the shepherd are invited to partake; and the murmuring of the scribes, who resented Christ's reception of sinners, is shown to be the opposite of the true spirit, which rejoices with heaven over the recovered. Gill's reading, the witnesses observe, finds in the "home" both the church below, into which Christ brings his found ones, and heaven above, their final home, whither grace alone can carry them. And the "friends and neighbours," the expositors note (anticipating the next verse), are the angels and the company of heaven, called to share the joy. The verse thus presses the social and overflowing nature of grace's joy: the recovery of one sinner is a gladness that fills the shepherd's house and calls all his friends to rejoice — a rebuke to every loveless heart that, like the Pharisees, would begrudge the lost their welcome, and a foretaste of the joy in heaven the next verse declares.
7:
"I say unto you, that likewise joy shall be in heaven over one sinner that repenteth, more than over ninety and nine just persons, which need no repentance." The witnesses note the heavenly joy and the meaning of "just persons which need no repentance." The Reformed Gill takes the joy as "in the church below... or in heaven above, and among the angels there." Lightfoot explains the "just persons" by the Jewish usage: "all the good, and those that were to be saved at last, they called just persons" — the contrast being with open sinners. Augustine (cited on v.8) reads the ninety-nine of "those who, trusting in themselves, prefer themselves to sinners returning to salvation" — the self-righteous Pharisees who "need no repentance" only in their own conceit.

The witnesses note the striking comparison: more joy over the one repentant sinner than over the ninety-nine — not because the righteous are less loved, but because the recovery of the lost is the more wondrous and unexpected mercy.

So the witnesses make the verse the great declaration of heaven's joy over the penitent. The lesson the expositors draw is the surpassing gladness in heaven over a recovered sinner, and the right reading of the "just persons." The joy, the witnesses note, is heaven's own — the rejoicing of God and his angels over one sinner that repents, a joy greater than over the ninety-nine. This does not mean, the expositors press, that the righteous are less dear to God, but that, as Calvin will note, the deliverance of one who was lost and devoted to destruction displays God's mercy more brightly, and yields, "after the manner of men, a joy arising out of an unexpected good." As for the "ninety and nine just persons which need no repentance," the witnesses observe two readings. The plainer takes them as the truly righteous, who have not strayed and so occasion no such dramatic recovery. But Augustine and others, the expositors note, read them ironically of the self-righteous Pharisees, who "need no repentance" only in their own estimation, trusting in themselves and despising returning sinners — so that the verse rebukes the very murmurers, whose conceit of righteousness shut them out of the joy of heaven. Either way, the witnesses press, the point stands against the Pharisees: heaven rejoices over the repentant sinner they despised, and to grudge that joy is to set oneself against the gladness of God and his angels. The verse thus crowns the first parable with its application — the joy of heaven over one sinner that repents — and presses every hearer to share, not grudge, that joy.
8:
"Either what woman having ten pieces of silver, if she lose one piece, doth not light a candle, and sweep the house, and seek diligently till she find it?" The witnesses note the second parable and its rich allegory. Chrysostom reads the coin as the image of God in man: "the race of man is compared to a piece of silver which was lost, by which he shows that we were made according to the royal likeness and image of the most high God; for the piece of silver is a coin having the impress of the king's image." Gregory the Great identifies the woman with God's wisdom: "He who is signified by the shepherd is also by the woman — for it is God himself, God and the wisdom of God." Gregory reads the ten pieces as "nine orders of angels, but that the number of the elect might be filled up, man the tenth was created." Augustine reads the nine, like the ninety-nine, as "those who, trusting in themselves, prefer themselves to sinners returning."

The witnesses note the woman's diligence: she lights a candle, sweeps the house, and seeks carefully until she finds the one lost coin.

So the witnesses make the verse the second parable of the lost, the coin bearing the King's image. The lesson the expositors draw is the worth of the lost soul and the diligence of the search. The silver coin, the witnesses note, is the apt emblem of man: stamped with the image of God, the King, as a coin bears the royal likeness — so that the lost sinner, fallen and defaced as he is, yet retains the dignity of one made in God's image, precious to his Maker and worth the seeking. The expositors press the figure of the woman: the fathers read her as God himself, or the divine Wisdom, or the Church, who, having lost one of the ten, does not despise the loss but searches with all diligence — lighting the candle (the light of the word, or of Christ), sweeping the house, and seeking carefully "till she find it." The patristic counting of the ten, the witnesses observe — the nine orders of angels and man as the tenth, created to complete the number of the elect — is a devout meditation marking the place of redeemed humanity among the heavenly host. And the diligence of the search, the expositors note, answers the murmuring Pharisees as the shepherd's pursuit did: the recovery of the lost is no trifle but a labour of love, pursued with light and care until the precious thing is found. The verse thus deepens the trilogy's theme — the lost is sought because it is precious, bearing the King's image — and prepares for the joy that the finding of the coin, like the finding of the sheep, will call forth in heaven.
9:
"And when she hath found it, she calleth her friends and her neighbours together, saying, Rejoice with me; for I have found the piece which I had lost." The witnesses note the woman's shared joy, paralleling the shepherd's. The grammatical-historical Robertson marks a careful detail: "her friends and neighbours" are female (the feminine article and word for friends), fitting the housewife; and "which I lost" — "she lost the coin (note the article); the shepherd did not lose the one sheep." The repetition of the call to "rejoice with me," the witnesses note, echoes the shepherd's words and presses the same lesson of communal joy over the recovered.

The witnesses note the woman, like the shepherd, cannot contain her joy but summons her friends to rejoice with her over the one coin found.

So the witnesses make the verse the second sharing of joy over the lost recovered. The lesson the expositors draw is the same overflowing gladness, now in the housewife's mouth: the finding of the lost is an occasion of festal joy, too great to keep private, calling others to share it. Robertson's small observation, the witnesses note, is suggestive: "which I had lost" — the woman owns the loss as her own, where the shepherd did not say the sheep lost itself; in both, the seeker takes responsibility for the recovery, the grace lying wholly on the seeking side. And the joy, the expositors press, is again communal: "she calleth her friends and her neighbours together" — the recovered coin, like the recovered sheep, fills the finder's house with gladness and draws others into it. The witnesses observe the deliberate parallel between the two parables: shepherd and housewife, sheep and coin, search and finding, and now the same call to shared rejoicing — driving home, by repetition, the single lesson that the recovery of the lost is heaven's joy, in which all are bidden to partake. The verse thus answers the Pharisees' murmuring a second time: where they grudged Christ's reception of sinners, the woman rejoices and calls others to rejoice over the one coin found; and the parable presses that to begrudge such joy is to be out of tune with heaven, which delights in the recovery of every precious soul that bore, however defaced, the image of its King.
10:
"Likewise, I say unto you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner that repenteth." The witnesses note the application and the precise location of the joy. The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the wording: "joy arises... in the presence of the angels of God — that is to say, the joy of God himself; the angels are in a sense the neighbours of God." Calvin answers why the one repentant yields more joy: "though it would be more agreeable that men should always remain in perfect integrity, yet in the deliverance of a sinner, who had been already devoted to destruction and cut off as a rotten member, the mercy of God shines more brightly; he attributes to God, after the manner of men, a joy arising out of an unexpected good." Barnes cross-references God's own declaration that he has "no pleasure in the death of the wicked," but desires that he turn and live (Ezekiel 18:23, 32; 33:11; Acts 11:18).

The witnesses note the joy is "in the presence of the angels" — not merely the angels' joy, but God's own joy, displayed before the heavenly host.

So the witnesses make the verse the climactic application of the second parable. The lesson the expositors draw is that the repentance of one sinner is the joy of God himself, witnessed by all heaven. The phrase, the witnesses note, is precise: not simply "joy among the angels," but "joy in the presence of the angels of God" — which Robertson reads as the joy of God himself, displayed before his angelic court, the angels being as it were the neighbours summoned to share his gladness, answering the friends and neighbours of the two parables. The expositors press Calvin's resolution of the apparent paradox: the greater joy over the one repentant does not mean the righteous are less prized, but that the recovery of a soul already devoted to destruction, snatched as a brand from the burning, displays the mercy of God more brightly and stirs, after the manner of men, the gladness of an unlooked-for good. And this joy, the witnesses observe, is grounded in the very heart of God, who "hath no pleasure in the death of the wicked" but rejoices when the lost turns and lives (Ezekiel 33:11). The verse thus seals the trilogy's central truth: the conversion of one sinner is the joy of heaven and of God, before all the angels — and the murmuring Pharisees, who grudged the publicans their Saviour, are shown to be at odds with the gladness of God himself. To receive sinners, the expositors note, is not Christ's shame but heaven's joy; and the verse calls every hearer to share that joy rather than, like the elder brother soon to appear, to resent it.
11:
"And he said, A certain man had two sons." The witnesses introduce the parable of the prodigal and note the unity of the three. Ambrose draws together the trilogy: "St. Luke has given three parables successively — the sheep which was lost and found, the piece of silver lost and found, the son who was dead and came to life again — that, invited by a threefold remedy, we might heal our wounds. Christ as the Shepherd bears you on His own body, the Church as the woman seeks for thee, God as the Father receives you: the first, pity; the second, intercession; the third, reconciliation." The witnesses note the "two sons" — the younger, who will stray and return, and the elder, who will represent the murmuring Pharisees — both addressed in the one parable.

The witnesses note that this third and greatest parable rises above the other two: where they pictured a sheep and a coin, this pictures a son — a rational, beloved child, capable of guilt and of penitence.

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the parable of the prodigal son. The lesson the expositors draw is the deepening of the trilogy's theme and the breadth of its scope. The three parables, the witnesses note with Ambrose, form a threefold remedy for the soul's wounds: the Shepherd's pity that bears the sheep, the Church's diligent seeking of the coin, and now the Father's reconciling embrace of the son — each revealing another aspect of the grace that receives sinners. The expositors press the advance: the lost sheep strayed by heedlessness, the lost coin by mischance, but the lost son strays by deliberate, willful departure — and so the third parable reaches deepest, picturing the sinner not as a wandering animal or a fallen object, but as a beloved child who knowingly forsakes his father, and who must be received not merely by being found and carried, but by repentance and forgiveness. The "two sons," the witnesses observe, embrace the whole audience: the younger figures the publicans and sinners drawing near to Christ, the elder the murmuring Pharisees who grudge them welcome; so that the parable both vindicates the reception of the penitent and rebukes the self-righteous who resent it. The verse thus opens the most beloved of Christ's parables, in which the heart of the Father toward returning sinners is unveiled as nowhere else in Scripture — the third and crowning answer to those who murmured, "This man receiveth sinners."
12:
"And the younger of them said to his father, Father, give me the portion of goods that falleth to me. And he divided unto them his living." The witnesses expound the younger son's demand and its meaning. The Reformed Gill reads the younger son as "God's chosen ones among the publicans and sinners," fitly so figured "since man, as a sinner, is younger than man as righteous," and since God often chose the younger before the elder (Jacob before Esau); the young man's character marks the elect "in a state of nature" — "imprudent and ignorant... yet conceited of themselves, fancying themselves wise, not caring to be under restraints, withdrawing from all yokes." Ambrose defends the father's giving: "nor think it wrong in the father that he gave it to the younger, for no age is weak in the kingdom of God; faith is not weighed down by years."

The witnesses note the demand: the younger son asks his portion now, impatient of his father's house, eager to be his own master — the very picture of the sinner who would have God's gifts but not God's rule.

So the witnesses make the verse the sinner's self-willed departure from God. The lesson the expositors draw is the restless independence of the natural heart, which would take God's gifts and be free of God's authority. The younger son's demand, the witnesses note, is the demand of the sinner: "give me the portion that falleth to me" — he wants the inheritance, the goods, the endowments of life, but he wants them in his own hand, to dispose as he pleases, free of his father's house and rule. The expositors press what this reveals: the natural man, endowed by God with reason, strength, and substance, is impatient of restraint, conceited of his own wisdom, and eager to be independent — to live for himself, away from his Father, master of his own portion. The father's granting of the request, the witnesses observe, is significant: "he divided unto them his living" — God, in his sovereignty, gives men the gifts and powers of life and even permits them to take their own course, not forcing obedience, but leaving the willful to the freedom they crave, that they may learn, in a far country, the bitterness of life without him. Ambrose's note, the expositors observe, guards the father from blame: it was no fault to give, for the gifts of God are freely bestowed. The verse thus pictures the first step of the sinner's ruin — the self-willed grasping of God's gifts apart from God himself — the heart that says, in effect, "give me what is mine, and let me be free of thee"; and it prepares for the far country, the waste, and the famine that such independence must bring.
13:
"And not many days after the younger son gathered all together, and took his journey into a far country, and there wasted his substance with riotous living." The witnesses expound the departure and the waste. Chrysostom reads the "far country" spiritually: "the younger son set out into a distant country, not locally departing from God, who is everywhere present, but in heart; for the sinner flees from God that he may stand afar off." The Reformed Gill applies it: "as soon as a wicked man has of this world what his carnal heart desires, he is for living independent of God... gathering all for himself, in order to spend it on his lusts, at a distance from his father." Lightfoot considers and rejects identifying him with the rebellious son of Deuteronomy 21:18.

The witnesses note the swift descent: gathering all, journeying far, and wasting all in riotous living — the rapid course of sin from independence to ruin.

So the witnesses make the verse the sinner's plunge into a far country and a wasted life. The lesson the expositors draw is the nature of departure from God and the swift wasting of his gifts. The "far country," the witnesses note with Chrysostom, is not a place but a state of heart: the sinner does not flee God by distance — for God is everywhere — but in disposition, turning his heart away, living as though God were far off, that he may sin without restraint. The expositors press the speed and completeness of the ruin: "not many days after," the son gathers all and departs — for the heart, once independent, does not linger but hastens into license; and "there wasted his substance with riotous living" — squandering in dissipation the gifts of God meant for better ends. Gill's application, the witnesses observe, traces the pattern of the natural man: given his desire, he lives for himself, far from his Father, spending the endowments of life — reason, strength, opportunity, substance — upon his lusts. The expositors note the tragedy of the waste: the same gifts that, used in the Father's house, would have enriched the son, are riotously consumed in the far country, leaving him poorer than before. The verse thus pictures the full course of the sinner's flight: the heart's departure into the far country of godless living, and the reckless wasting of all God gave — the prelude to the famine and want that such a life must end in. It is the portrait of every soul that takes God's gifts and spends them in forgetfulness of the Giver, hastening from independence to destitution.
14:
"And when he had spent all, there arose a mighty famine in that land; and he began to be in want." The witnesses note the emptiness of sin and the famine that exposes it. The Reformed Gill draws the spiritual sense: "sin strips a man of all that is good and valuable — of the image of God, the knowledge of divine things, natural holiness, moral righteousness, and strength to perform moral good; hence man is in a wretched and miserable condition, poor, blind, and naked." He presses the doctrinal point: "if man has spent all, and sin has stripped him of all, where is his free will? there is no good thing in man but what comes from the grace of God." Barnes cross-references the deeper famine of the soul — a famine "of hearing the words of the Lord" (Amos 8:11-12).

The witnesses note the double calamity: the son had spent all, and then a famine arose — so that his poverty was met by a land that could not feed him, and he "began to be in want."

So the witnesses make the verse the point of the prodigal's destitution. The lesson the expositors draw is the utter emptiness to which sin reduces the soul. The son, the witnesses note, "had spent all" — the gifts of God, wasted in riotous living, were now gone, leaving him stripped and bare; and Gill presses the spiritual reality this figures: sin robs a man of every good — the image of God, the knowledge of divine things, righteousness, strength — leaving him poor, blind, and naked, with nothing of his own to fall back on. The expositors note the doctrinal weight Gill draws: if sin has spent all and stripped the sinner of all good, then there is no native goodness or free will in fallen man to recommend him to God; whatever good he may yet attain must come wholly from grace, for of himself he has nothing. The "mighty famine," the witnesses observe, deepens the ruin: not only had the son spent all, but the land itself failed, so that even had he kept his goods he could not have been sustained — a picture, the expositors note, of the world's utter inability to satisfy the soul, which, having forsaken God, finds in the far country no bread, only a famine that exposes its want. Barnes's reference to the famine of the word, the witnesses note, hints at the deeper hunger: the soul away from God starves for the very thing the far country cannot give. The verse thus brings the prodigal to the beginning of his extremity — all spent, the land barren, and want closing in — the point where sin's promise of freedom is unmasked as bondage and beggary, and the soul is brought low enough to begin, at last, to think of home.
15:
"And he went and joined himself to a citizen of that country; and he sent him into his fields to feed swine." The witnesses note the prodigal's degrading servitude and a striking allegorical reading. The Reformed Gill rejects several identifications of the "citizen" — not a saint, nor the devil (who is more than a citizen, being the prince of this world), nor likely one a convicted sinner would seek — and proposes instead "an unregenerate, pharisaical, legal preacher": "a man may be a preacher and yet in the far country of sin... large gifts where there is no grace... a form of godliness without the power; and it is common for persons under legal convictions to seek after such." Barnes takes it more plainly: he "entered the service of that citizen, hired himself out, even to the lowest kind of work."

The witnesses note the depth of the fall: the son who had lived in riot now hires himself to a stranger to feed swine — the most degrading work, abhorrent to a Jew, for whom the swine was unclean.

So the witnesses make the verse the bottom of the prodigal's degradation. The lesson the expositors draw is the bondage and shame into which sin's freedom betrays the soul. The son who had grasped his portion to be his own master, the witnesses note, now finds himself a servant — "joined himself to a citizen of that country," reduced from a son's liberty to a hireling's servitude, and that of the meanest kind. The expositors press the irony: he had fled his father's house to escape restraint, and has found a harsher master; he had sought freedom, and has found the most degrading bondage. The swine-feeding, the witnesses observe, marks the depth of his fall: for a Jew to tend swine, the unclean beast, was the lowest of degradations — the picture of the sinner sunk to serve his basest lusts, in employment utterly beneath the dignity of one made in God's image. Gill's distinctive reading, the expositors note, finds in the "citizen" the legal, unregenerate preacher to whom the awakened sinner first turns — seeking relief from his want by the dry husks of mere moralism and self-righteous religion, which cannot feed the soul; a striking, if particular, application of the far country's barren service. Whether read so, or simply as the degrading servitude of sin, the verse pictures the same truth: the prodigal, having spent all, is reduced to a shameful bondage that cannot satisfy — the soul that fled God now enslaved in the far country, feeding swine, and still hungry. The verse thus brings the sinner to the lowest point of his ruin, where the next verse shows him longing for the very husks the swine eat.
16:
"And he would fain have filled his belly with the husks that the swine did eat: and no man gave unto him." The witnesses note the prodigal's extreme hunger and the failure of the world to feed him. The Reformed Gill identifies the "husks" as the fruit of the carob tree, "the food of beasts," and reads them spiritually as "worldly riches and honours, and carnal lusts and pleasures... but swine's meat, very mean food, yea pernicious, empty, unsatisfying, and perishing." Barnes notes "he would fain — he would gladly" have eaten even this, "the husks" being the pods with little nourishment. The witnesses note the bitter touch "no man gave unto him": in the far country, none would even give him the swine's food — the world that had welcomed his money now abandons him in his want.

The witnesses note the depth of his misery: reduced to envying the swine their husks, and denied even these, the prodigal is utterly destitute and friendless.

So the witnesses make the verse the prodigal's deepest want, where the far country's emptiness is fully exposed. The lesson the expositors draw is the inability of the world and sin to satisfy the soul, and the friendlessness of the sinner in his ruin. The son, the witnesses note, is brought so low that he "would fain have filled his belly with the husks that the swine did eat" — longing for the very fodder of the unclean beasts, so desperate is his hunger. Gill's spiritual reading, the expositors press, finds in the husks the things of the far country — worldly pleasures, riches, and lusts — which are but swine's meat: mean, empty, unsatisfying, perishing food that can never fill the soul made for God. The sinner, the witnesses observe, having forsaken the Father's plenty, tries to feed his hunger on these husks, but they cannot satisfy; the soul starves amid the world's pleasures, for they are not its proper bread. And the bitter words "no man gave unto him," the expositors note, complete the desolation: the far country, which flatters and welcomes while the money lasts, abandons the ruined sinner in his want — the false friends of riotous living give nothing in the day of need; the world is a hard master that takes all and gives nothing back. The verse thus brings the prodigal to the very bottom of his misery — hungry, friendless, envying the swine, denied even husks — the extremity that, by God's grace, becomes the turning-point; for it is from this depth of unsatisfied want, where the far country has nothing left to offer, that the next verse shows him coming to himself and remembering his father's house, where even the hired servants have bread enough and to spare.
17:
"And when he came to himself, he said, How many hired servants of my father's have bread enough and to spare, and I perish with hunger!" The witnesses dwell on the turning-point of the parable. Gregory of Nyssa marks the change: "broken down by hardship... eating the same food with the swine, he returned, chastened, to his father's house." Ambrose draws out the phrase: "he rightly returns to himself, because he departed from himself; for he who returns to God restores himself to himself, and he who departs from Christ rejects himself from himself." Augustine adds: "he returned to himself when, from those things which without unprofitably entice and seduce, he brought back his mind to the inward recesses of his conscience." The witnesses note the comparison that pierces him: his father's hired servants have bread to spare, while he, a son, perishes with hunger.

The witnesses note that "he came to himself" — the first stirring of repentance, the awakening of conscience and reason after the madness of sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the dawning of repentance in the prodigal. The lesson the expositors draw is the nature of conversion as a coming to oneself and a remembering of the Father's bounty. The phrase "he came to himself," the witnesses note with the fathers, is profound: sin is a kind of madness, a departure from one's true self, in which a man, made for God, lives below his nature and against his reason; and repentance is the return of the soul to itself — the conscience awakening, the mind brought back from the empty enticements of the far country to its inward recesses. Ambrose's insight, the expositors press, is exact: in departing from his father the son had departed from himself, lost his true identity as a son; in returning to God he restores himself to himself, for the soul is only truly itself when it is with its Maker. The means of this awakening, the witnesses observe, was misery: the famine, the swine, the husks denied — the bitter want of the far country broke his pride and drove him to think. And the first thought of his returning mind, the expositors note, is the contrast that pierces him: in his father's house, even the hired servants — the lowest, the day-labourers — "have bread enough and to spare," while he, a son, perishes with hunger. This memory of the Father's plenty, set against his own destitution, is the spark of repentance: the recollection that there is bread, and to spare, in the house he forsook. The verse thus marks the great turn of the parable — the sinner coming to himself, awakening from the madness of sin, and remembering, in his extremity, the abundance of the Father's house; the first step of the long road home.
18:
"I will arise and go to my father, and will say unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and before thee." The witnesses note the resolution of repentance and the confession it frames. The grammatical-historical Barnes draws out "I will arise" as "a common expression to denote entering on a piece of business... he meant immediately to return; this should be the feeling of every sinner conscious of his guilt." He presses the only refuge: "To my father — he has nowhere else to go but to God; he has offended him, but he may trust in his kindness; if God does not save him he cannot be saved." Gill marks the completeness of the resolve: the son determines "to quit the country itself," leaving the citizen, the swine, and the husks. The confession "I have sinned against heaven, and before thee" the witnesses note acknowledges sin as against God first and the father second.

The witnesses note the decisiveness: not a vague wish but a settled resolution — "I will arise and go" — joined to a humble confession of sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the resolve and confession of the penitent. The lesson the expositors draw is the nature of true repentance: a decisive turning to the Father, joined to honest confession of sin. The prodigal, the witnesses note, does not merely lament his misery nor wish vaguely for better days, but forms a definite resolve — "I will arise and go to my father" — the soul rousing itself from the far country to return to God. Barnes presses the wisdom of the choice, the expositors observe: the sinner has nowhere else to go; he has offended God, yet God is his only hope, for none else can save — so the awakened soul, conscious of guilt, turns not from God but to him, trusting in a kindness it has wronged. Gill notes the completeness of the resolve, the witnesses observe: the son will quit not merely his sins but the far country itself, leaving the citizen, the swine, and the husks — a thorough forsaking of the life of sin, not a half-return. And the confession the son frames, the expositors press, is the language of genuine penitence: "I have sinned against heaven, and before thee" — owning his sin first as against God ("heaven"), the deepest offence, and then against his father; taking the blame wholly upon himself, with no excuse. This, the witnesses note, is the heart of repentance — not self-justification but self-accusation, the honest confession of guilt to the One sinned against. The verse thus frames the penitent's return: a decisive resolve to arise and go to the Father, and a humble confession of sin against heaven and against him — the words in which every returning sinner finds his own.
19:
"And am no more worthy to be called thy son: make me as one of thy hired servants." The witnesses note the depth of the prodigal's humility. The Reformed Gill marks the self-abasement: the son "now sees, and was ready to own his unworthiness to be called" a son "in any sense." Barnes draws out the threefold spirit of the request: "Treat me as a servant... I do not ask to be treated as a son." This evinces, he notes, "1st, deep humility, such as a sinner should have; 2nd, love for his father's house, such as all penitents should have toward God; 3rd, confidence in his father that he would treat him kindly, even if as a servant — such confidence all returning penitents feel in God."

The witnesses note the son no longer claims the rights of a son, but, counting himself unworthy, would be content with the place of a hired servant in his father's house.

So the witnesses make the verse the lowliness of true repentance. The lesson the expositors draw is the deep humility, love, and confidence that mark the returning penitent. The son, the witnesses note, having framed his confession, adds the measure of his self-abasement: "am no more worthy to be called thy son" — he renounces all claim to a son's place, counting himself unworthy of the relation he had despised; and "make me as one of thy hired servants" — he would be content with the lowest place in his father's house, asking not for sonship but for service. The expositors press Barnes's threefold analysis: this is genuine humility, such as becomes a sinner conscious of his guilt, who feels he has forfeited every right and deserves nothing; it is love for the father's house, such as all true penitents feel toward God, longing to be near him even in the meanest place; and it is confidence in the father's kindness, the assurance that even as a servant he would be treated better than in the far country — the trust that all returning sinners feel in God. The witnesses observe the beauty of the disposition: the son does not bargain, does not plead his rights, does not excuse himself, but casts himself wholly on his father's mercy, content to be anything in the house he had left. This, the expositors note, is the spirit the gospel commends — the broken and contrite heart that, claiming nothing, trusts all to grace. Yet the parable will show, the witnesses press, that the father's love far exceeds the son's request: he asks to be a servant, and is received as a son — for grace gives more than the penitent dares to ask. The verse thus completes the prodigal's resolve in the lowliest humility, the very temper to which the Father's overflowing welcome will answer.
20:
"And he arose, and came to his father. But when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him." The witnesses dwell on the father's running compassion. The Reformed Gill marks that the resolve was carried out by grace: "this shows that his resolution to arise was not of nature, but of grace, by its being put into execution; for it was made and executed, not in his own strength, but in another's." Barnes paints the scene: "a great way off — the image of his father's happening to see him clad in rags, poor, and emaciated, and yet he recognized his son, and all the feelings of a father prompted him to go and embrace him... And ran — this is opposed to the manner in which the son came," for the father hastens where the son had come slowly, driven by want and shame.

The witnesses note the father's eagerness: he sees the son afar off, is moved with compassion, runs to meet him, embraces and kisses him — anticipating the son's confession with his welcome.

So the witnesses make the verse the heart of the parable: the father's overflowing welcome of the returning son. The lesson the expositors draw is the eager, anticipating love of God toward the penitent. The son, the witnesses note, "arose, and came" — and Gill marks that this carrying-out of the resolve was itself the work of grace, not of the son's own strength; for the will to return and the power to return are alike God's gift. But the wonder of the verse, the expositors press, is the father: "when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him" — the father was watching, looking for the son's return, his heart turned homeward toward the wanderer; and seeing him afar off, ragged and ruined, he "had compassion, and ran" — the aged father, casting aside all dignity, runs to meet the returning sinner. The witnesses observe the tender details: he does not wait for the son to reach him, nor demand explanation, nor receive him coldly, but runs to him, "fell on his neck, and kissed him" — the embrace of full and free forgiveness, given before the son has spoken a word of his confession. This, the expositors note, is the very picture of God's grace toward the returning sinner: he meets the penitent while he is yet far off, runs to him in compassion, and embraces him with forgiving love before he can finish his confession. The contrast Barnes draws, the witnesses press, is moving: the son came slowly, heavy with shame; the father ran, swift with love — so that the welcome outpaces the return. The verse thus unveils the heart of God in the running, kissing father: not a reluctant pardon wrung from a stern judge, but the eager, anticipating embrace of a Father who has longed for his child's return and rejoices to receive him.
21:
"And the son said unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in thy sight, and am no more worthy to be called thy son." The witnesses note the son's confession, and a striking omission. The Reformed Gill notes the son, overwhelmed by the kiss, "seems to be prevented making this request" — the plea to be a hired servant. Adam Clarke presses the textual and theological point: the words "make me as one of thy hired servants," added here in some manuscripts, are "evidently added merely to make his conduct agree with his resolution; but by this a very great beauty is lost: for the design is to show... the great affection of the father, and his readiness to forgive. His tenderness of heart cannot wait till the son has made his confession; his bowels yearn over him, and he cuts short his tale of contrition by giving him the most plenary assurances of his pardoning love."

The witnesses note that the son confesses his sin and unworthiness, but does not get to ask for the servant's place — the father's love interrupting before the request can be made.

So the witnesses make the verse the confession that grace cuts short. The lesson the expositors draw is the overflowing forgiveness of the Father, which outruns the penitent's plea. The son, the witnesses note, having been embraced and kissed, pours out the confession he had prepared: "Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in thy sight, and am no more worthy to be called thy son" — owning his guilt and his unworthiness as he had resolved. But, the expositors press with Clarke, he does not finish: he had meant to add "make me as one of thy hired servants," but the words are not spoken — the father's love interrupts before the request can be made. This omission, the witnesses observe, is no defect but the very beauty of the parable: the father will not hear of his son being a servant; his tenderness cannot wait for the full tale of contrition, but cuts it short with the assurance of pardoning love, commanding (in the next verse) the robe and the ring. The expositors note the lesson: grace gives far more than the penitent dares to ask. The son came intending to beg the lowest place, content to be a hireling; but the father, overruling his request, restores him fully as a son — for God does not receive the returning sinner grudgingly, as a servant on probation, but freely, as a child, clothing him in the best and killing the fatted calf. The witnesses press the comfort: the penitent need not fear that God will hold him at arm's length; the Father's love anticipates and exceeds his humblest hopes, cutting short his self-abasement to lavish upon him the full blessings of sonship. The verse thus shows the confession received and the servant's plea silenced by grace — the returning sinner forgiven more freely and fully than he had dared to ask.
22:
"But the father said to his servants, Bring forth the best robe, and put it on him; and put a ring on his hand, and shoes on his feet." The witnesses expound the tokens of full restoration. The Reformed Gill marks the grace in the opening word: "the word 'but'... is expressive of much grace; it signifies that though the son had behaved so badly, and desired to be a hired servant, the father would hear nothing of it." The grammatical-historical Barnes expounds each gift: "the best robe" is "the outer garment, reserved for festival occasions" (Genesis 27:15); "a ring on his hand" was "a mark of wealth and dignity... a mark of favour, or of affection, or of conferring office" (Genesis 41:42; James 2:2); "shoes on his feet" — for "servants did not usually wear shoes; the son returned without shoes," so to shoe him was to treat him not as a slave but as a free son.

The witnesses note the father gives commands that overrule the son's request: not the rags of a hireling, but the robe, the ring, and the shoes of a restored son.

So the witnesses make the verse the full restoration of the penitent to sonship. The lesson the expositors draw is the completeness and freeness of the grace that receives the returning sinner. The father, the witnesses note, does not answer the son's plea to be a servant, but commands his servants to bring the tokens of full sonship — and Gill marks the eloquent "but," the grace that hears nothing of the son's self-abasement but lavishes upon him the blessings of a child. Each gift, the expositors press, carries its meaning. The "best robe" — the festal garment, the finest in the house — figures, the witnesses observe, the robe of Christ's righteousness, in which the pardoned sinner is clothed, covering all his rags and shame. The "ring on his hand" is the token of dignity, favour, and adoption — the seal of restored sonship, marking him not a servant but a son and heir. The "shoes on his feet" distinguish the free son from the barefoot slave, fitting him to walk in his father's house and ways. The witnesses note the lavishness of the welcome: the son had returned in rags, expecting at most a servant's place; the father clothes him in the best, rings his hand, shoes his feet — restoring him fully and freely, with every mark of honour. This, the expositors note, is the gospel: the returning sinner is not received on probation, nor kept at a distance, but justified, adopted, and adorned — clothed in righteousness, sealed as a son, made meet to walk with God. The verse thus unveils the riches of grace toward the penitent: the Father's love overrules the sinner's lowest hopes, and restores him not to servitude but to the full dignity and joy of sonship.
23:
"And bring hither the fatted calf, and kill it; and let us eat, and be merry." The witnesses note the feast and the rich allegory of the fatted calf. The Reformed Gill offers a twofold reading: "by which Christ is designed, in allusion to the calves offered in sacrifice... so Christ has offered up himself, in the room and stead of his people, an offering and sacrifice to God of a sweet smelling savour... and made full atonement and expiation for all their sins"; or, "in allusion to the feasts and liberal entertainments of friends, Christ is the best provision that can be set before a believer, or he can feed upon — true and real food, spiritual, savoury, satisfying." Barnes notes "be merry" is literally "eating, let us rejoice."

The witnesses note the killing of the fatted calf — the choicest of the herd, kept for the greatest occasions — as the centre of the festal joy over the son's return.

So the witnesses make the verse the festal joy of the father's house. The lesson the expositors draw is the abundant provision and rejoicing with which God celebrates the return of the penitent. The fatted calf, the witnesses note, was the choicest beast, kept and fattened for the most joyful and solemn occasions; its killing marks the depth of the father's gladness — nothing is too good for the returned son, and the best of the house is brought forth to celebrate. Gill's twofold allegory, the expositors press, finds in the fatted calf a figure of Christ himself: either as the sacrifice, slain in the sinner's stead to make atonement, on whom the pardoned feast; or as the spiritual food, the rich provision set before the believer, on whom the soul feeds and is satisfied. Either reading, the witnesses observe, joins the feast to Christ: the returning sinner is welcomed to a table whose provision is the Saviour, slain for him and given to him — so that the joy of restoration is a feast upon Christ, the bread of life and the Lamb of sacrifice. The "let us eat, and be merry," the expositors note, sets the tone of heaven's response to repentance: not grudging reluctance, but festal rejoicing, the whole house called to share the gladness. The witnesses press the contrast with the elder son soon to appear: where the father commands a feast and merriment over the recovered sinner, the elder brother will resent the very joy — the same contrast as between heaven's gladness and the Pharisees' murmuring. The verse thus brings the parable to its festal height: the fatted calf killed, the table spread, the house bidden to rejoice — the abundant, Christ-centred joy with which God receives every returning sinner.
24:
"For this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found. And they began to be merry." The witnesses note the father's reason for the feast and the double sense of "dead" and "lost." The Reformed Gill expounds the spiritual death: before, the son "was dead — dead in Adam, dead in law, being under a sentence of condemnation, and dead in trespasses and sins"; a death which "lies in a separation from God... in alienation from the life of God, in the deformation of his image, in the loss of original righteousness." Barnes notes the two senses of "dead": either "I supposed that he was dead, but know now that he is alive," or "he was dead to virtue, sunk in pleasure and vice," and is now restored. The witnesses note the father acknowledges the returned penitent as "my son" — owning the relation the son had thought forfeited.

The witnesses note the joy is grounded in a resurrection: the son once dead is alive, once lost is found — the recovery of one given up for gone.

So the witnesses make the verse the ground and the substance of the festal joy. The lesson the expositors draw is that conversion is a passage from death to life, from lostness to recovery, and so an occasion of the deepest joy. The father, the witnesses note, gives his reason for the feast: "this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found" — and the words carry, the expositors press, the whole doctrine of conversion. The sinner, away from God, is "dead": dead in trespasses and sins, separated from the life of God, his image defaced, under condemnation — a spiritual death as real as the death of the body. And he is "lost": wandered, gone, given up for ruined. But in returning to God, the witnesses observe, he passes from death to life — quickened by grace, made alive in Christ — and from lostness to recovery, found by the seeking love that the whole chapter has displayed. This passage from death to life, the expositors note, is the greatest of changes, and so the fittest occasion of the greatest joy: the father rejoices not over a son who never strayed, but over one raised, as it were, from the dead, recovered from the grave of sin. The witnesses note the tender "my son" — the father owns the relation the son had renounced, restoring him fully to the place of a child. And "they began to be merry," the expositors observe, marks the joy beginning in the father's house — the very joy of heaven over one sinner that repents, now pictured in the festal gladness over the son raised and found. The verse thus crowns the parable's first movement: the dead alive, the lost found, the house merry — the joy of God over the returning sinner, grounded in the resurrection from sin to life that conversion is.
25:
"Now his elder son was in the field: and as he came and drew nigh to the house, he heard musick and dancing." The witnesses introduce the elder son and his allegorical meaning. Bede sets the turn: "in the two first parables He hints at the joy He has with the angels; but in the third He not only declares His joy, but also blames the murmurings of those who were envious — the elder son." Augustine reads the elder son as "the people of Israel, not gone into a distant country, yet not in the house, but in the field, that is, in the paternal wealth of the Law and the Prophets, choosing to work earthly things"; the music and dancing he reads of "men filled with the Holy Spirit, with harmonious voices preaching the Gospel."

The witnesses note the elder son was "in the field" — at his work, dutiful in outward service — and hears, on returning, the sounds of the feast: music and dancing.

So the witnesses make the verse the entrance of the elder brother, who figures the self-righteous. The lesson the expositors draw is the introduction of the murmuring spirit that the parable will expose and rebuke. The elder son, the witnesses note, represents — as the fathers and the moderns agree — the Pharisees and scribes whose murmuring occasioned the whole chapter (Luke 15:2), and behind them the people of Israel, who, having the law and the prophets, grudged the Gentiles and the publicans their welcome into grace. He was "in the field," the expositors press: at his work, outwardly dutiful, serving (as the next verses show) in a servile spirit, yet not enjoying the intimacy of the house. Augustine's reading, the witnesses observe, finds him in the field of the law's earthly labours, not in the freedom of the house; and the music and dancing he hears, the joyful sounds of the feast, figure the gladness of the gospel, the Spirit-filled rejoicing of the Church over returning sinners. The verse thus turns the parable from the joy of the father over the younger son to the resentment of the elder — from the celebration of grace to the murmuring against it. The witnesses note the importance of this second movement: the parable does not end with the prodigal's welcome, but goes on to expose the elder brother's grudging, that the murmuring Pharisees might see themselves in him, and learn how their resentment of grace set them against the joy of their Father. The verse thus prepares for the elder son's anger and the father's tender appeal — the rebuke of the self-righteous who, dutiful in the field, cannot rejoice at the sinner's return.
26:
"And he called one of the servants, and asked what these things meant." The witnesses note the elder son's inquiry and its spirit. The Reformed Gill identifies the servant as "one of the ministers of the word, one of the disciples of Christ, as the scribes and Pharisees sometimes did, choosing rather to speak to one of the disciples than to Christ himself when they were offended." The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the servant here is paidōn (a serving-boy), not doulos (bondslave) as in verse 22, and that the elder son "inquired" (imperfect) — "repeatedly and eagerly" — "what these things might be."

The witnesses note the elder son does not go in to see, but stops outside and questions a servant — eager to know, yet keeping his distance from the feast.

So the witnesses make the verse the elder son's distant, suspicious inquiry. The lesson the expositors draw is the alienation of the self-righteous spirit, which inquires about grace from afar rather than entering into its joy. The elder son, the witnesses note, hearing the music and dancing, does not go in to share the gladness, but "called one of the servants" and asked what it meant — keeping outside, questioning at a remove. Robertson's note on the eager, repeated inquiry, the expositors observe, marks his agitation: something is happening in his father's house that he does not understand and already half-resents. Gill's reading, the witnesses press, finds in the servant questioned a minister or disciple of Christ — for the Pharisees, when offended, often turned to question the disciples rather than Christ himself (as elsewhere in the Gospels); so the elder brother's recourse to a servant figures the self-righteous seeking explanation of the gospel's joy from those around Christ. The verse thus marks the elder son's characteristic posture, the witnesses note: he is near the house but not in it, hearing the joy but not joining it, inquiring about grace but holding himself apart from it. This, the expositors press, is the spirit of the Pharisee: dutiful in outward service, yet a stranger to the joy of his Father; near enough to hear the music of grace, but too proud or too cold to enter the feast. The verse thus prepares for his anger: having learned that the feast is for his returned brother, the elder son will refuse to go in — the self-righteous resentment of grace now coming to the surface, that the father's tender appeal will seek to overcome.
27:
"And he said unto him, Thy brother is come; and thy father hath killed the fatted calf, because he hath received him safe and sound." The witnesses note the servant's report and its tender terms. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the phrase "safe and sound" — "literally, in health" — the same participle "characteristic of St. Luke and St. Paul" (Luke 5:31; 7:10), the physician's word. Robertson notes the verbs: "is come" (a perfect sense), "hath killed" (did kill), "hath received" — literally "hath gotten him back," the prefix marking recovery. Wesley observes the servant "mentions this [the fatted calf] rather than the robe or ring, as having a nearer connection with the music and dancing."

The witnesses note the servant's report names the son as "thy brother" — the very relation the elder son will refuse to own — and gives the father's reason: joy at receiving him back safe and sound.

So the witnesses make the verse the servant's account of the feast. The lesson the expositors draw is the warmth of the household's joy over the recovered son, set against the elder brother's coming coldness. The servant, the witnesses note, reports the matter plainly and tenderly: "thy brother is come" — naming the returned prodigal as the elder son's brother, the relation of kinship and love; "thy father hath killed the fatted calf" — the token of the deepest joy; "because he hath received him safe and sound" — the father's reason, gladness that the lost is restored in health. The expositors press the significance of "safe and sound": Luke the physician's word marks the son's recovery not only of body but of soul, brought back whole from the ruin of the far country. The witnesses observe the contrast the verse sets up: the servant speaks of "thy brother" with natural warmth, but the elder son (in v.30) will refuse the word, calling him only "this thy son" — the loveless heart denying the kinship that grace had restored. And the father's reason, the expositors note, is pure joy: he killed the fatted calf not because the son had earned it, but because he was received back safe — the gladness of recovery, not the reward of merit. The verse thus reports the household's festal joy in tender terms, preparing for the jarring contrast of the elder son's anger: where the servant says "thy brother" and tells of the father's gladness, the elder brother will answer with resentment and disdain — the self-righteous heart unable to share the joy that fills the Father's house over a sinner come home safe and sound.
28:
"And he was angry, and would not go in: therefore came his father out, and intreated him." The witnesses note the elder son's anger and the father's gracious appeal. The Reformed Gill reads the anger as the self-righteous resentment of free grace: "the preaching of a crucified Christ, and of free and full salvation by him, is highly displeasing to self-righteous persons; they are angry that it should be free, that election should be of grace, that justification should be freely by the grace of God, and that pardon should be according to the riches of his grace... that it should be for sinners, and alone by Christ." Clarke connects it directly: "this refers to the indignation of the scribes and Pharisees" (Luke 15:1-2). The witnesses note the father comes out and entreats the angry son — the same condescension shown to the younger now extended to the elder.

The witnesses note the elder son refuses to go in, and the father, rather than commanding, comes out and pleads with him — grace stooping to the self-righteous as to the sinner.

So the witnesses make the verse the elder son's resentful anger and the father's tender entreaty. The lesson the expositors draw is the self-righteous heart's hatred of free grace, and the Father's condescension even to the resentful. The elder son, the witnesses note, "was angry, and would not go in" — refusing to share the joy, resenting the welcome given to his returned brother. Gill's exposition, the expositors press, lays bare the root: this is the anger of self-righteousness at the freeness of grace — the displeasure of those who, trusting in their own works, cannot bear that salvation should be free, full, and given to sinners; the Pharisees murmured at Christ's receiving publicans, and the elder son figures their resentment exactly. The witnesses observe what offends him: not that his brother had sinned, but that his brother is freely forgiven and feasted — the grace that asks no merit, the welcome that crowns no works. This, the expositors note, is the abiding scandal of the gospel to the self-righteous: that God justifies the ungodly freely, and rejoices over the returning sinner without regard to his deserts. Yet the father, the witnesses press, does not abandon the angry son: "therefore came his father out, and intreated him" — the same love that ran to meet the prodigal now goes out to plead with the elder, stooping to entreat where he might have commanded. The expositors note the tenderness: God deals graciously even with the self-righteous, going out to reason with them, seeking to win them from their resentment into the joy of the house. The verse thus shows the two sons answered by the one love: the younger embraced in his return, the elder entreated in his anger — the Father seeking both, and grieved that the dutiful should grudge the joy that grace has brought.
29:
"And he answering said to his father, Lo, these many years do I serve thee, neither transgressed I at any time thy commandment: and yet thou never gavest me a kid, that I might make merry with my friends." The witnesses note the elder son's self-justifying complaint. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the telling word: "the very word 'I serve,' as a slave serves, is eminently suggestive — the obedience had all along been servile, prompted by fear and hope" of reward, not love. Clarke exposes the injustice of the complaint: "it is evident the father gave him his portion when his profligate brother claimed his... and though he had not claimed it, he might have done so whenever he chose; therefore his complaining was both undutiful and unjust." Barnes notes the "kid" was of less value than the fatted calf, the elder son grudging that he never had even the lesser.

The witnesses note the elder son boasts of his long, faultless service, yet complains he was never given even a kid — measuring his father's love by reward.

So the witnesses make the verse the elder son's self-righteous self-justification. The lesson the expositors draw is the servile, mercenary, and loveless spirit of self-righteousness. The elder son, the witnesses note, answers his father not with affection but with a catalogue of his own merits: "these many years do I serve thee, neither transgressed I at any time thy commandment" — boasting of a long and (in his own estimate) faultless obedience, the very temper of the Pharisee who trusts in himself that he is righteous. Ellicott's observation, the expositors press, exposes the heart of it: the word he uses is "I serve, as a slave serves" — his obedience had been servile all along, rendered from fear and the hope of reward, not from love; he had been in his father's house as a hireling in spirit, not a son. And his complaint, the witnesses observe, reveals the mercenary root: "thou never gavest me a kid" — he measures his father's love by what he has received, grudging that his long service was never rewarded with even a small feast, while his brother's return is crowned with the fatted calf. The expositors note the injustice Clarke marks: the father had divided his living, so all that remained was the elder son's own; he had wanted nothing, and might have had a feast whenever he chose — his complaint is groundless as well as loveless. The verse thus lays bare the self-righteous heart: dutiful in outward service yet servile and joyless within, trusting in its own obedience, measuring God's love by reward, and resenting the grace shown to others. It is the portrait of the Pharisee, and a warning to every heart that serves God as a slave for wages rather than as a son in love.
30:
"But as soon as this thy son was come, which hath devoured thy living with harlots, thou hast killed for him the fatted calf." The witnesses note the elder son's contempt and the venom of his words. The grammatical-historical Barnes marks the disdain: "this thy son — this son of thine; this is an expression of great contempt; he did not call him his brother, but his father's son, to show at once his contempt for his younger brother, and for his father for having received him." The Reformed Gill reads the offence as resentment at free grace to sinners: he was offended "that a crucified Christ should be preached... and much less that he should be preached to sinners, and still less that he should be preached so soon to them, as soon as ever they became sensible of their sin."

The witnesses note the elder son will not own his brother — "this thy son" — and casts up his sins, resenting that such a one should be received and feasted.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax of the elder son's resentment. The lesson the expositors draw is the loveless contempt of the self-righteous toward the returning sinner. The elder son, the witnesses note, refuses to acknowledge his brother: "this thy son" — not "my brother," as the servant had said, but "this son of thine," a phrase, Barnes presses, of studied contempt, disowning the kinship that grace had restored and reproaching the father for receiving him. He casts up the worst of his brother's sins — "which hath devoured thy living with harlots" — dwelling on the shame, exaggerating perhaps, to make the welcome seem the more unjust; the self-righteous heart, the expositors observe, magnifies the sinner's faults to justify its own resentment of his pardon. And the true offence, the witnesses note with Gill, is the grace itself: that such a one should be received at all, and so freely, and so soon — for the Pharisee cannot bear that the worst of sinners should be welcomed the moment he repents, without the long penance and proving the self-righteous would impose. The verse thus exposes the ugliness of the elder brother's spirit: cold to his brother, reproachful to his father, contemptuous of grace, and unable to rejoice in a salvation that asks no merit. It is the mirror of the murmuring Pharisees, who grudged the publicans their Saviour; and it stands as a warning that the elder-brother spirit — resentful of free grace to others, proud of its own service, and loveless toward the lost — may shut a man out of the Father's joy as surely as the prodigal's riot once shut him out of the Father's house. The verse thus brings the elder son's complaint to its bitter height, that the father's answer may rebuke and yet still woo him.
31:
"And he said unto him, Son, thou art ever with me, and all that I have is thine." The witnesses note the father's tender answer to the elder son. The grammatical-historical Barnes expounds the truth of it: "the property was divided; what remained was in reality the elder son's; he was heir to it all, and had a right, if he chose, to use it; he had therefore no right to complain." Barnes draws the parable's design: "the elder son represents the Pharisees; the younger, the returning sinner, whether Jew or Gentile; and the father, God, who is willing to receive them." The witnesses note the tender address "Son" — the father still owns the resentful elder as his child, and reminds him of his constant privilege.

The witnesses note the father does not rebuke harshly but reasons gently: the elder son has always had his presence and all his goods — he has lost nothing by his brother's return.

So the witnesses make the verse the father's gentle vindication and appeal. The lesson the expositors draw is the constant privilege of those who abide with God, and the unreasonableness of grudging grace to others. The father, the witnesses note, answers the elder son's bitter complaint not with anger but with tenderness: "Son" — still owning him as a child, despite his resentment — "thou art ever with me, and all that I have is thine." The expositors press the truth and the appeal in these words.
