The truth: the elder son had lost nothing; the property was already divided, and all that remained was his; he had enjoyed, all along, the constant presence of his father and the use of all his goods — privileges far greater than a kid or a feast. His complaint, the witnesses observe, was therefore groundless: he had not been wronged by his brother's welcome, for his own portion and place were untouched. The appeal: the father reminds him of what he has, that he might cease to grudge what his brother has received — for the joy over the returned prodigal takes nothing from the elder, who has always had the father's presence, the best of all gifts. The witnesses note the application Barnes draws: the elder son is the Pharisee, who had the law, the covenant, the constant privileges of God's people, and yet grudged the grace shown to publicans and Gentiles — forgetting that his own standing was secure, and that the joy over returning sinners diminished nothing of his portion. The verse thus answers the self-righteous resentment with a reminder of unfailing privilege: those who abide with God lack nothing, and have no cause to envy the grace shown to the returning lost. It is the Father's gentle reasoning with the elder-brother heart, that it might lay down its resentment and enter the joy.
32:
"It was meet that we should make merry, and be glad: for this thy brother was dead, and is alive again; and was lost, and is found." The witnesses note the father's final word, pressing the duty of joy and restoring the word "brother." The Reformed Gill notes the father teaches the elder son the rightness of the joy: "it was meet that we should make merry — both father, son, and servants; and this elder brother also, because of the relation he stood in to him; and if he had had the same spiritual affection the apostle had for his brethren, he would have rejoiced at the conversion of sinners." Adam Clarke marks the father's gentle correction: "to awaken this ill-natured, angry man to a sense of his duty, this amiable father returns him his own unkind words, but in a widely different spirit: This son of mine is Thy brother, to whom thou shouldst show tenderness... he was dead in sin, is quickened by the power of God; he was lost, but now is found." The witnesses note the parable ends here, on the note of joy and the appeal to the elder son, without recording his response.

The witnesses note the father insists the joy was fitting and necessary — "it was meet" — and calls the prodigal "thy brother," correcting the elder son's "this thy son."

So the witnesses make the verse the parable's closing appeal: the duty and rightness of joy over the returning sinner. The lesson the expositors draw is that to rejoice over the recovered lost is fitting, necessary, and the mark of a true child of the Father. The father, the witnesses note, presses the propriety of the celebration: "it was meet that we should make merry, and be glad" — not merely permissible but right and necessary, the only fitting response to so great a mercy; and he grounds it, as before, in the resurrection from sin: "this thy brother was dead, and is alive again; and was lost, and is found." Clarke marks the tender correction, the expositors press: the father takes up the elder son's own scornful words and gently turns them — where the elder said "this thy son," the father says "this thy brother," restoring the kinship the elder had denied, and reminding him that the returned prodigal is his own brother, to whom he owes tenderness, not contempt. The witnesses observe the searching application to the murmuring Pharisees: they ought to have rejoiced at the conversion of publicans and sinners, who were their brethren; their grudging resentment showed them out of tune with the joy of their Father, and lacking the love that should mark his children. And the parable, the expositors note, ends here — open, unresolved, without recording whether the elder son went in or stayed out; for the question is left hanging before the Pharisees themselves, and before every reader: will you enter the joy of the Father over returning sinners, or stand outside in self-righteous resentment? The verse thus closes the trilogy of grace with its tenderest and most pointed appeal: it is meet to rejoice over the lost restored, for the dead is alive and the lost is found — and the Father's joy is the measure and the invitation of his children's.
